posts.raw_content:
1.  He will do it by changing the value of the outstanding treasury bills ~70 cents on the dollar (this will knock 30% of the debt down to 28T)

2. When people panic a dump treasuries Bessent will print 2T and purchase 4-5T and retire the bonds (It will cause some inflation, but the debt will drop further 3-4T to 25T)

3. This will fuck the cities, democrat hell holes, the democrat union pensions and other democrat money holes that the democrats use to purchase voters.

4. The cities will have to cut spending and benefit packages for teachers and other state government grifters (if they cut for cops, cops walk and then the cities become lord of the flies). The Democrat run cities own a disproportionate amount of treasuries and are the perfect places to eat the debt.

5. He will argue Fraud has been an unfair burden on the American taxpayer, and we should not have to carry the future obligations through to the youth.

6. He will argue to the globe that he solved (x) number of crises (Iran, Cuba, Isis, Abraham Accords, Maybe Ukraine, Venezuela, many of which will be resolved by next Feb 2027), and that the world owes some debt forgiveness.

7. There will be a market correction when announced, it will be severe, then the U.S. will exist with ~25T in debt and lower debt service.  Boomer bonds will be impacted, but the overall impact will be lower than hyper-inflation and when the market corrects non-pavnicans will recover.

8. Congress will be forced to reduce budget deficits (including debt service interest), to sub 500B deficits, 5T over ten years, 10T over twenty.

All of these actions will buy the U.S. 10-20 years of economic prosperity, with the potential for more if we can continue to elect MAGA patriots.

*If something like this isn't the plan, then I don't see how we get past Hyper-inflation, I think much of what Trumps agenda has been is to get around congresses inaction on financial matters.  I would be surprised if the national debt isn't his end game, seeing as how he has managed debt his entire professional career,

posts.raw_content: Ohio House Bill 854 proposes to tax high-earning residents to completely fund free college tuition for in-state students. Introduced in May 2026 by Democratic State Representatives Munira Abdullahi and Tristan Rader, the legislation aims to establish the Ohio Free College Fund to eliminate financial barriers to higher education.Key Details of the LegislationFree Tuition Guarantee: The bill mandates that all 14 of Ohio's public universities provide free undergraduate tuition for up to eight semesters to any qualifying in-state resident.The Funding Mechanism: It creates a progressive state income tax tier specifically targeting individuals, trusts, and estates making more than $500,000 annually.Progressive Tax Rates: The new tax applies only to income earned above the baseline:Income between $500,000 and $1 million would be taxed at 5.375%.Income exceeding $1 million would be taxed at 7.375%.Additional Revenue Sources: The text of OH HB 854 on BillTrack50 also outlines a 1% fee on residential property transfers valued over $2 million and a tax on corporate employers for compensation exceeding $10 million annually.Estimated Fiscal ImpactProponents of the bill estimate that roughly 2.38% of Ohio households earn above the $500,000 threshold. Lawmakers project that these changes could generate roughly $3.125 billion annually. Based on current averages for in-state public tuition, this revenue would be sufficient to fund tuition for more than 244,000 students, covering the entire current in-state public university population.Current Status and OutlookThe bill was officially referred to the House Workforce and Higher Education Committee on May 13, 2026, where it awaits its initial hearing dates. Because the Ohio General Assembly holds a firm Republican supermajority, political analysts expect the bill faces incredibly steep hurdles to passing. Critics frequently argue that such measures increase the tax burden, risk driving wealthy residents out of state, and question the overall return on investment compared to funding K-12 systems.

posts.content: Ohio House Bill 854 proposes to tax high-earning residents to completely fund free college tuition for in-state students. Introduced in May 2026 by Democratic State Representatives Munira Abdullahi and Tristan Rader, the legislation aims to establish the Ohio Free College Fund to eliminate financial barriers to higher education.Key Details of the LegislationFree Tuition Guarantee: The bill mandates that all 14 of Ohio's public universities provide free undergraduate tuition for up to eight semesters to any qualifying in-state resident.The Funding Mechanism: It creates a progressive state income tax tier specifically targeting individuals, trusts, and estates making more than $500,000 annually.Progressive Tax Rates: The new tax applies only to income earned above the baseline:Income between $500,000 and $1 million would be taxed at 5.375%.Income exceeding $1 million would be taxed at 7.375%.Additional Revenue Sources: The text of OH HB 854 on BillTrack50 also outlines a 1% fee on residential property transfers valued over $2 million and a tax on corporate employers for compensation exceeding $10 million annually.Estimated Fiscal ImpactProponents of the bill estimate that roughly 2.38% of Ohio households earn above the $500,000 threshold. Lawmakers project that these changes could generate roughly $3.125 billion annually. Based on current averages for in-state public tuition, this revenue would be sufficient to fund tuition for more than 244,000 students, covering the entire current in-state public university population.Current Status and OutlookThe bill was officially referred to the House Workforce and Higher Education Committee on May 13, 2026, where it awaits its initial hearing dates. Because the Ohio General Assembly holds a firm Republican supermajority, political analysts expect the bill faces incredibly steep hurdles to passing. Critics frequently argue that such measures increase the tax burden, risk driving wealthy residents out of state, and question the overall return on investment compared to funding K-12 systems.
